sources.verification_method: Founder narrative: David Cramer's personal blog 'Sentry: From the Beginning' documents the project's history. Origin of the open-source code is 2008 (Cramer's unlicensed Django plugin), but Functional Software, Inc. — the legal entity currently operating Sentry — was incorporated separately. Per METHODOLOGY 7.1, founded_year tracks the legal entity, not the project. Cra.mr is the founder's personal blog (source.type='other' because it does not fit company-website / tech-press / press-release enums; it is a primary first-person founder source which is the strongest available narrative for company-founding history).

Combined with the Cramer narrative (Heroku AddOn launch end-of-2012, 'David founded Sentry in January 2012'), founded_year=2012 is the best available triangulated estimate. Per METHODOLOGY 3.2, ≥2 independent sources for the high-stakes claim. CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM. Sentry's current LICENSE.md (HEAD master, browser-validated by Pablo on 2026-05-09) declares 'Copyright 2008-2024 Functional Software, Inc. dba Sentry'. The 2008 start of the copyright claim reflects the original year of the open-source code (assigned to the entity at incorporation), NOT the year the entity itself was incorporated. Atlas uses 2012 per METHODOLOGY 7.1 ('Year of legal incorporation of the jurisdictional entity that currently operates the company'). Pablo's ruling on 2026-05-09 explicitly chose 2012 over 2008 to maintain consistency with MongoDB (founded_year=2007 = 10gen Inc. incorporation) and CockroachDB (founded_year=2015 = Cockroach Labs Inc. incorporation), both of which apply 7.1 strictly to legal entities, not to project origins. METHODOLOGY 7.1 NOT amended — this case is canonical 7.1 application, not an edge requiring rule extension.

license_history.rationale_public: Per Sentry's 2019-11-06 'Re-Licensing Sentry' announcement (David Cramer): the relicense was driven by the same free-rider problem that Cockroach Labs cited five months earlier (June 2019) — cloud providers and competing observability vendors offering Sentry-as-a-service or rebadged Sentry monitoring without contributing back. BSL-1.1 with Sentry's chosen 36-month conversion to Apache-2.0 forbids commercial competing offerings during the conversion window while remaining free for end-user / internal-deployment use; converted code is automatically Apache-2.0 after the conversion period.

Per Sentry's 2023-11-20 'Introducing the Functional Source License' announcement (David Cramer): the BSL Additional Use Grant mechanism had developed inconsistent semantic interpretations across implementers (MariaDB, Redis, HashiCorp each defining 'competing use' differently), creating user confusion and legal uncertainty. Sentry developed FSL as a successor to BSL with three explicit changes: (a) shorter conversion delay (24 months vs 36 months on Sentry's prior BSL configuration); (b) a fixed 'Permitted Purpose' clause replacing the negotiable Additional Use Grant; (c) per-version conversion semantics with explicit SPDX-style license identifier (FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 or FSL-1.1-MIT). FSL is published openly at fsl.software for adoption by other companies; Liquibase has subsequently adopted FSL.

sources.verification_method: Live LICENSE.md file at master HEAD of getsentry/sentry, browser-validated by Pablo on 2026-05-09 (Chrome screenshot). Additional triangulation: TechCrunch 2023-11-20 (Frederic Lardinois, contemporaneous with announcement), The Register 2023-11-20, InfoQ 2023-12, fsl.software (Sentry's own FSL spec hosting page).

SPDX-canonical short form: 'BSD-3-Clause' (not bare 'BSD-3' — schema vocabulary uses BSD-3-Clause per controlled-vocabularies.yaml line 139).

Per METHODOLOGY 3.2: ≥2 independent sources for high-stakes claim. Additional corroboration: The Register 2023-11-20, InfoQ 2023-12, fsl.software.

Current LICENSE.md is a single FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 covering all main Sentry web app code; no clause offers an alternative concurrent license that the user can elect. The FSL time-delayed conversion to Apache-2.0 (effective 24 months after each version is made available) does NOT make this dual licensing per METHODOLOGY 7.9 strict definition ('same source code offered under two concurrent licenses, with the user choosing which one applies'). Per METHODOLOGY 7.18 (added 2026-05-09 in commit c820708): 'time-delayed automatic conversion does not count as dual_license=true unless the same current source code is simultaneously offered under user-selectable alternative licenses' — this is the canonical Atlas application of 7.18. The conversion mechanism (per-version, 24 months, post-conversion = Apache-2.0) is documented in metadata.notes.

Sentry's CONTRIBUTING.md (browser-fetched 2026-05-09 via web_fetch) is intentionally minimal — 11 lines, 8 LOC, 379 bytes, two sections: 'Feedback, Advice, and General Help' (directing to GitHub Discussions) and 'Contributing to Sentry' (linking to docs.sentry.io/internal/ for server development documentation). NO mention of Contributor License Agreement (CLA), Developer Certificate of Origin (DCO), sign-off requirement, or out-of-band contributor agreement page. Per METHODOLOGY 7.10 hierarchy: item (1) out-of-band company legal page = NOT found (sentry.io/legal/ surfaces DPA but no contributor agreement); item (2) visible CLA bot integration on PRs = NOT confirmed (cla-assistant.io/getsentry/* endpoint not surfaced via WebSearch; no CLA Assistant or EasyCLA bot integration found in repo workflows surfaced via search); item (3) CONTRIBUTING.md statement = explicit absence; item (4) DCO sign-off = not mentioned. Conclusion: cla_required=false. CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM. The absence of evidence at hierarchy items 1, 3, 4 plus silence at item 2 is consistent with cla_required=false but is not as strong as positive confirmation via a sample PR review showing no bot intervention. TODO post-Free-Preview: Pablo browser-validate a recent PR in getsentry/sentry to confirm absence of CLA-bot intervention; also check develop.sentry.dev internal docs (which CONTRIBUTING.md links to) for any contributor-agreement language. Methodologically distinct from MongoDB (out-of-band CLA via legal page) and CockroachDB (CLA Assistant bot integration) — Sentry takes a third position consistent with its public anti-CLA posture (FSL design rationale explicitly cites avoiding contributor-permanent-assignment via time-delayed Apache-2.0 conversion).

Sacra private-market research profile for Sentry identifies multiple parallel commercial offerings as of 2024-2025: (1) Sentry Cloud SaaS at sentry.io with free tier + paid self-serve tiers (Team starts at USD 26/mo, Business, Enterprise); (2) Self-hosted Sentry under FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 (free for non-Competing-Use deployments, no revenue threshold gating — distinct from CockroachDB CSL which has a USD 10M revenue cap on the free self-hosted tier); (3) Enterprise self-hosted contracts with named enterprise customers (GitHub, Disney, Atlassian, Reddit, Slack referenced by Sacra; Microsoft, Dropbox, Disney by Contrary Research). Three+ material parallel offerings places Sentry into 'hybrid' per METHODOLOGY 2.1 — and 2.1 explicitly lists Sentry as a canonical example of the 'hybrid' enum. EDGE CASE NOTE per METHODOLOGY 2.1 strict definition: 'hybrid' assumes the existence of an OSS layer ('OSS + hosted + enterprise tiers'). Post-2023-11-20, Sentry has NO OSS layer in the OSI sense — FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 is source-available proprietary with the time-delayed Apache-2.0 conversion. The OSS layer technically does emerge per-version after the 24-month conversion delay (versions older than 24 months ARE Apache-2.0), but the HEAD/latest GA release that anyone forking today encounters is FSL — that is the canonical license per METHODOLOGY 7.18. Sentry classified 'hybrid' in Atlas v1 as a least-bad fit, identical resolution to CockroachDB post-CSL (see CockroachDB metadata.notes item 14). The case reinforces the schema TODO from METHODOLOGY section 8 for a v1.1 enum value 'source-available-tiered' to capture the FSL/CSL/SSPL/Elastic-2.0 pattern more precisely without forcing the 'hybrid' fit.

Contrary Research Sentry Business Breakdown independently confirms the 'hybrid open-source and self-serve SaaS business model' classification, citing the same multi-offering structure: (a) free open-source SDKs (which are MIT/Apache permissive, NOT FSL — the SDKs are the developer-facing gateway product); (b) free FSL self-hosted Sentry server; (c) Cloud SaaS with free + paid tiers; (d) enterprise contracts. NOTE on multi-repo license heterogeneity: while the main getsentry/sentry repo is FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0, the SDKs (sentry-python, sentry-javascript, sentry-java, sentry-cocoa, sentry-ruby, sentry-laravel, sentry-react-native, sentry-dotnet, etc.) are typically Apache-2.0 or MIT permissive — this is the cara pública del producto for developers and is intentional product strategy: the SDKs are designed to embed in user code, so they need to be permissive for adoption, while the server-side aggregation/storage is gated commercially. This pattern was flagged during Phase 2 pre-research and is documented here in commercial_model.type rather than license fields because 'license heterogeneity across product surfaces' is fundamentally a commercial-model design choice, not a license-history fact about the canonical primary_repo.

Sacra private-market research explicitly states 'self-serve SaaS model accounts for 70% of Sentry's revenue', with the remaining ~30% from enterprise contracts and other sources. Self-serve Cloud SaaS is the dominant revenue channel; this is the primary_revenue_stream. CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM (single private-market research source for the exact 70% split; Sentry has not publicly disclosed its revenue mix as a private company). Schema enum 'cloud' applied per the dominant 70% Cloud SaaS revenue. Cross-record observation: MongoDB primary_revenue_stream='cloud' (Atlas SaaS dominant); CockroachDB primary_revenue_stream='enterprise-license' (self-hosted majority); Sentry primary_revenue_stream='cloud' — Sentry matches MongoDB pattern (cloud-dominant), distinct from CockroachDB pattern (self-hosted-dominant). This is methodologically meaningful for Hypothesis 4 (or whichever hypothesis tests revenue-mix patterns by category): databases skew enterprise-license, observability skews cloud — early N=3 signal worth tracking.

Contrary Research independently corroborates the Cloud-dominant revenue thesis: 'Sentry employs a hybrid open-source and self-serve SaaS business model. The self-serve model accounts for 70% of Sentry's revenue' (matching Sacra's figure exactly — likely both citing the same upstream Sentry investor-deck data point or each other; this is not 2 fully independent confirmations of the 70% figure but rather 2 sources reporting the same number from a shared origin). Sacra additionally reports unverified ARR estimate of USD 128M at end of 2023 with 30% YoY growth — this is the order-of-magnitude commercial scale context, NOT a high-confidence ARR claim (Sentry has not publicly disclosed ARR; both Sacra and Contrary mark ARR figures as estimates).

pricing.free_tier_notes: Two distinct free tiers exist as of 2026-05-09: (1) Sentry Cloud Developer plan: USD 0/month, 5,000 errors and 10,000 performance units (transactions/spans) per month, 30-day data retention, 1 user, 1 project (per pricing-research aggregators 2026). Designed for individual developers and evaluation. (2) Self-hosted Sentry under FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0: free indefinitely for non-Competing-Use deployments at any company size. Per METHODOLOGY 7.18, the FSL converts each release per-version to Apache-2.0 after 24 months — so the self-hosted free option layers a per-version-converted Apache layer underneath the FSL HEAD. This is materially distinct from CockroachDB's CSL Free tier which requires a License Key + mandatory Telemetry sharing + USD 10M revenue cap (a USD 50M-revenue startup running self-hosted Sentry pays USD 0; the same startup running CockroachDB CSL would require paid Enterprise licensing).
pricing.self_hosted_restrictions: Self-hosted Sentry under the Functional Source License v1.1, Apache 2.0 Future License (FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0, master HEAD post-2023-11-20) is free for any deployment that satisfies the 'Permitted Purpose' definition. Restrictions documented in the LICENSE.md file at github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/master/LICENSE.md (browser-validated by Pablo on 2026-05-09): (a) Permitted Purpose = any purpose other than a 'Competing Use'; Competing Use = making the Software available to others in a commercial product or service that substitutes for any other product or service offered using the Software (broadly: cannot launch a Sentry-as-a-service offering competing with sentry.io). (b) Internal use, internal modifications, prototyping, development, even production internal deployments are all permitted regardless of company size or revenue. (c) Per-version time-delayed conversion: each commit/release independently converts to Apache-2.0 on its second anniversary, per METHODOLOGY 7.18; no manual action required to access the converted version (clone the repo and checkout a release tag from >24 months ago = Apache-2.0). (d) NO License Key required (distinct from CockroachDB CSL). (e) NO mandatory Telemetry / phone-home sharing (distinct from CockroachDB CSL). (f) NO revenue threshold or seat cap (distinct from CockroachDB CSL's USD 10M revenue gate and from n8n's Sustainable Use License which gates internal-use vs external-use). (g) FSL is NOT OSI-approved open source; the Permitted Purpose carve-out is the source-available-vs-OSS distinction. The functional permissiveness is materially closer to a permissive OSS license (Apache-2.0) than to a typical source-available license (BSL-1.1, CSL, Elastic-2.0, SSPL) — distinguished primarily by the explicit Competing Use restriction and the 24-month delay before becoming literal Apache-2.0.

sources.verification_method: Sentry pricing page lists Developer plan as 'Free $0' (the leftmost free tier with 5,000 errors and 10,000 performance units monthly, 30-day data retention, 1 user, 1 project). Cross-confirmed by Sentry Help Center Free Plan FAQ (sentrypricing.com/free-plan, Sentry-aligned aggregator). NOTE: pricing-page direct browser validation by Pablo is recommended pre-Phase-7 (the cowork-egress allowlist permitted earlier sentry.io URLs in this session via WebSearch; sentry.io is reachable for some endpoints but pricing-page render specifics may vary).

Self-hosted Sentry under FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 (master HEAD, browser-validated by Pablo) is free indefinitely for any non-Competing-Use deployment with no revenue threshold. This is a second, structurally-distinct free tier from the Cloud Developer plan: Cloud Developer is a free SaaS slot with usage caps; self-hosted FSL is a free deployment-everywhere option with the Permitted Purpose carve-out. Per Atlas convention (MongoDB precedent free_tier_notes documents both Atlas Free + Community Edition; CockroachDB precedent free_tier_notes documents both Cloud Basic + CSL Free), Sentry free_tier_notes documents both Cloud Developer + self-hosted FSL.

FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 LICENSE.md text (browser-validated by Pablo on 2026-05-09). The license grants 'an additional license to use the Software under the Apache License, Version 2.0 that is effective on the second anniversary of the date the Software is made available'. License Grant section permits use, copy, modify, derivative works, public performance, public display, redistribution for 'any Permitted Purpose' (= any purpose other than a Competing Use). Self-hosted free deployment is therefore indefinite for non-Competing-Use users at any company size. self_hosted_free_indefinite=true is correct in the dataset semantic ('free for some users indefinitely'); the Permitted Purpose / Competing Use restriction is captured in self_hosted_restrictions.

Sentry pricing page lists three GA self-serve paid Cloud tiers as of 2026-05-09: Team starting at USD 26/mo (annual billing) or USD 29/mo (monthly billing); Business starting at USD 80/mo (annual) or USD 89/mo (monthly); Enterprise (sales-led, custom-only). Per METHODOLOGY 7.17, cloud_entry_price_usd_month captures the lowest fixed-price paid GA cloud tier. Atlas convention: capture the headline annual-commit price (Team USD 26/mo) which is what sentry.io presents prominently on the pricing page; the monthly alternative (USD 29/mo) is the no-commitment option visible on the toggle. Documented in metadata.notes as a single-tier capture with annual/monthly variant. CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM pending browser validation by Pablo of the live pricing page numbers (the pricing-aggregator sources [vendr, g2, costbench, sentrypricing.com, last9, bugsly, nurbak, capterra] all converge on USD 26/mo Team annual; Sentry Help Center FAQ confirms USD 26 annual / USD 29 monthly base for Team and USD 80 annual / USD 89 monthly base for Business; no PREVIEW status flagged on any tier per METHODOLOGY 7.17 amendment). Cross-confirmed by sentry.zendesk.com Aug 27 2025 plan-change FAQ which states the base prices remained unchanged across the volume-restructuring announcement. NO non-GA tier exclusion required. Pablo browser-validation of sentry.io/pricing recommended pre-Phase-7 to upgrade MEDIUM → HIGH (analog to CockroachDB Phase 4 browser validation 2026-05-05).

Sentry Help Center 2025-08-27 FAQ on plan restructuring confirms 'the base price of Team ($29 USD) and Business ($89 USD) plans remained unchanged' across the August 2025 event-volume restructuring. The USD 29 figure is the monthly-billing variant; the annual-commit USD 26 figure is the headline on the public pricing page. Per Atlas convention (capture annual-commit headline), USD 26 is captured; the monthly-billing USD 29 alternative is documented in metadata.notes.

Sentry pricing combines multiple obligatory billing dimensions: (1) plan tier base price (Team USD 26/mo annual or USD 80/mo Business annual = compute/seat/feature-tier dimension); (2) event volume = errors + transactions/spans + replays + attachments (event-based usage dimension, with included monthly quotas per tier and overage charges beyond, e.g., ~USD 0.00025/error and ~USD 0.000025/transaction overage rates); (3) data retention (default 30 days; longer retention available on higher tiers and as add-on). Multi-dimensional canonical pricing structure → 'hybrid' enum per METHODOLOGY 7.11 (≥2 mandatory or recurring dimensions in the canonical bill structure). Distinct from MongoDB Atlas (also hybrid: compute + storage + egress + backup) and CockroachDB Cloud (also hybrid: compute + storage + egress + add-ons). Cross-record observation: all three records pricing_model='hybrid'; the OSS-with-Cloud-SaaS pattern naturally produces multi-dimensional billing as the SaaS scales beyond a single base price.

Sentry Enterprise tier on the pricing page is sales-led ('Custom — Talk to sales' UI pattern). No public per-seat or per-event minimum disclosed for the Enterprise tier as of 2026-05-09. Set to null per Atlas convention; sales-led pricing is common for enterprise observability platforms (Datadog, New Relic, Splunk all follow the same pattern at the Enterprise tier). Cross-confirmed by absence of self-serve Enterprise pricing on sentry.io/pricing across all aggregator sources surveyed.

Sentry's self-serve range spans Developer (USD 0/mo) through Business (USD 80/mo annual base + event overage) — Business is the highest tier purchasable without sales contact. The 'self_serve_ceiling_usd' field is ambiguous for event-based pricing models because the actual top-of-self-serve depends on event volume consumed (a Business customer with high event volume could pay multiple thousands per month via overage, all self-served). Set to null and documented in metadata.notes per Atlas convention (event-based ceiling is not a single fixed-price figure).

feature_gating.notes: Sentry observability gates SSO/SAML, fine-grained RBAC (custom roles, team-level roles), audit logs, and tiered SLA support behind a commercial Business+/Enterprise tier relationship. multi_region_ha is OSS (NOT commercial) — distinct from MongoDB and CockroachDB precedent: Sentry democratizes data residency choice (US vs EU Frankfurt) across all Cloud tiers including the free Developer plan at no extra cost, and self-hosted FSL deployments have unrestricted multi-region freedom. The semantic distinction matters: in databases, multi_region_ha measures cluster cross-region survival goals (technically gated); in observability, multi_region_ha measures data residency / regional ingestion (democratized for GDPR/compliance reasons). White-label and API rate limits are not applicable to a developer-first observability product. Advanced analytics is partial: basic error tracking + basic Performance Monitoring + basic Session Replay are in Free; Custom Dashboards, advanced Performance Monitoring, full Session Replay, Profiling, AI Issue Grouping, and cross-project metrics are Business+/Enterprise-gated.

sources.verification_method: Sentry official SSO documentation states: 'SSO is available only if your organization is on a Business or Enterprise plan'. SAML2-based authentication is configurable via the generic SAML2 provider (docs.sentry.io/organization/authentication/sso/saml2/) and pre-built integrations exist for Okta, Azure AD, OneLogin. SCIM provisioning (docs.sentry.io/product/accounts/sso/okta-sso/okta-scim/) is also gated to Business+ tiers. Atlas value 'commercial': SSO/SAML requires Business or Enterprise tier subscription — not available on free Developer or paid Team tiers. Cross-confirmed by costbench.com Sentry pricing 2026 breakdown ('SSO is part of compliance features at Business tier and above') and Stitchflow Sentry user-management documentation.

Sentry Organization Member Roles documentation confirms a tiered RBAC structure: built-in organization-level roles (Owner, Manager, Admin, Member, Billing) are available in all paid tiers, but team-level roles, custom roles, and fine-grained permission templates require Business or Enterprise tier. The field 'rbac_fine_grained' specifically measures fine-grained capability (custom roles, team-level granularity), not the existence of any RBAC; per MongoDB and CockroachDB precedent, this maps to 'commercial' when the fine-grained capability is tier-gated even if basic RBAC is not. CONFIDENCE: HIGH for the gating fact (multiple sources confirm Business+ requirement); MEDIUM for naming the exact tier where each individual capability is unlocked (some sources cite Business, others cite Enterprise for specific custom-role features).

Sentry audit logging documentation indicates audit log access is gated to Business+/Enterprise tier (consistent with the SSO + custom RBAC compliance bundle at Business). Cross-confirmed by costbench.com Sentry 2026 pricing breakdown ('compliance features SSO + audit logs at Business tier') and SigNoz Sentry pricing guide. Atlas value 'commercial' applied: audit logging requires Business+ tier subscription, not available on Developer or Team.

Sentry offers tiered support coupled to commercial relationship: (a) Developer (free): community support — docs, GitHub Discussions, public forums, no SLA-backed response times; (b) Team: standard support; (c) Business: enhanced support with faster response (24x7 chat support per multiple aggregator sources); (d) Enterprise: dedicated Customer Success Manager + custom SLA contract terms negotiated per deal. Tier-based SLA-backed support is therefore a commercial feature (not present on Developer free tier without paying for a higher subscription). Atlas value 'commercial' applied. NOTE: Sentry has NOT publicly disclosed exact uptime-SLA percentage commitments per tier on the public pricing page (no '99.9% Business / 99.99% Enterprise' style explicit numbers found via WebSearch as of 2026-05-09); the SLA tier structure is presented qualitatively (community → standard → enhanced → dedicated). This contrasts with CockroachDB Cloud which publishes explicit uptime SLA per tier (99.99% Basic+Standard / 99.999% multi-region Advanced) on their pricing page.

Sentry's official Data Storage Location documentation states: 'When creating a Sentry organization, customers have the option of choosing either the US or the EU as a region for that organization.' The EU data center is in Frankfurt, Germany with backups within the EU. CRITICAL ATLAS DECISION: per sentry.zendesk.com 2024 EU Region FAQ and the Sentry Help Center pricing FAQ, EU Data Residency is offered to customers ON ALL PLAN TIERS AND AT NO EXTRA COST — including the free Developer plan, paid Team plan, Business plan, and Enterprise plan. This is materially distinct from MongoDB (multi-region clusters are Atlas Dedicated commercial only) and CockroachDB (multi-region survival goals require Enterprise license + License Key). Atlas value 'oss' applied: data residency / regional ingestion choice is democratized across the free tier and paid tiers alike. SEMANTIC NOTE: in databases, multi_region_ha measures cluster cross-region survival goals (technically gated as Enterprise feature). In observability, multi_region_ha measures data residency / regional ingestion architecture, which Sentry has democratized for GDPR / compliance reasons. The field semantics are distinct between categories — Atlas applies the field to its observability-relevant interpretation here rather than forcing the database interpretation. Further: self-hosted FSL deployments have UNRESTRICTED multi-region deployment freedom (the user can deploy Sentry server containers in any cloud region, in any topology, without License Key gating — distinct from CockroachDB CSL which requires License Key for multi-region survival goals). Both the Cloud and self-hosted dimensions of multi_region_ha are open/free for Sentry. CONFIDENCE: HIGH for the gating fact (sentry.zendesk.com explicit 'all plan tiers, no extra cost'); HIGH for the methodological decision (rule documented in metadata.notes and in this verification_method).

Sentry Help Center EU Region FAQ explicitly confirms: 'Sentry offers EU Data Residency (from Frankfurt, Germany) to customers on all plan tiers and at no extra cost. This includes the free Developer plan as well as Business and Enterprise tiers.' Independent corroboration of docs.sentry.io per METHODOLOGY 3.2.

Sentry is a developer-first error tracking and performance monitoring product, not an embeddable observability primitive that customers rebrand. White-label / OEM rebranding is not a user-facing product feature available through tier selection. Marked 'na' (not applicable to product type), consistent with MongoDB and CockroachDB precedent for non-OEM-eligible products. NOTE: Sentry SDKs are open-source and embedded into customer codebases under MIT/Apache permissive licenses — but the SDKs are integration code, not white-label product.

Sentry's analytics surface tiers as follows: (a) basic error tracking, basic Performance Monitoring, basic Session Replay — available on Developer (free) and Team tiers; (b) Custom Dashboards, advanced Performance Monitoring, full Session Replay (with longer retention and DOM/network/console enrichment), Profiling (UI profiling 'limited to higher paid tiers'), AI-powered Issue Grouping, cross-project metrics — available on Business+/Enterprise. Atlas value 'partial' applied: basic analytics in Free, premium analytics commercial-tier-gated. Consistent with MongoDB and CockroachDB precedent on advanced_analytics='partial' (basic vs commercial split). Cross-record consistency across all 3 verified records on this field.

Sentry exposes a REST API at sentry.io/api/ for programmatic interaction (project management, event querying, organization administration). API rate limits are inherent to the service architecture — they exist as protective rate-limiting on the API service, not as a 'feature unlock' that customers buy in higher tiers. The 'api_rate_limits' field is intended to capture whether higher rate limits are a feature gate (e.g., '1k req/sec on Free vs unlimited on Enterprise' as a marketed feature). Sentry does not market rate limits this way; rate limits are framed as a service-stability mechanism, not a tier feature. Marked 'na' (not applicable to product positioning), consistent with MongoDB and CockroachDB precedent.

strategic_signals.pricing_changes_l24m: Material pricing-model change inside the L24M window (cutoff 2024-05-09): on 2025-08-27 Sentry restructured event volume allocations across plan tiers while keeping the base subscription prices unchanged at Team USD 26/mo (annual) / USD 29/mo (monthly) and Business USD 80/mo (annual) / USD 89/mo (monthly). The restructuring affected included monthly quotas for errors, transactions/spans, replays, and attachments at each tier (per sentry.zendesk.com plan-change FAQ). NO changes to the base subscription price floor; NO changes to the Free Developer or Enterprise tier structures. The cloud_entry_price_usd_month=USD 26/mo captured in pricing has remained stable across the L24M window. Wayback Machine snapshots of sentry.io/pricing across L24M were not captured during this data collection (cookie/extension permission constraints during browser session); pricing-page diff documented qualitatively from the company's own change-log (sentry.zendesk.com 2025-08-27 FAQ + sentry.io changelog).

sources.verification_method: L24M cutoff = 2024-05-09 (data_collection_date 2026-05-09 minus 24 months). Per METHODOLOGY 7.16, license_changes_l24m captures only events inside the rolling 24-month window. Documented in license.license_history (where it canonically lives) but excluded from strategic_signals.license_changes_l24m. Field correctly empty. Cross-confirmed: no subsequent license change has been announced or committed between 2023-11-20 and 2026-05-09 (verified against blog.sentry.io, sentry.io/press-releases, getsentry/sentry repo LICENSE.md history surfaced in Phase 2 — same FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 since the 2023 transition).

Confirmation that LICENSE.md HEAD (browser-validated by Pablo on 2026-05-09) is FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 — the same license adopted on 2023-11-20. No subsequent license change inside the L24M window. Provided as a falsifiability anchor: a reader checking the license_history versus license_changes_l24m can verify directly that the 2023-11-20 FSL transition is the only candidate event, and it falls ~6 months outside the cutoff.

No subsequent VC/PE round disclosed through 2026-05-09 (verified against sentry.io press-releases page, blog.sentry.io, TechCrunch, PRNewswire, PitchBook 2026, Crunchbase, Tracxn 2026). Disney, Peloton, Cloudflare, Eventbrite, Slack, Supercell, Rockstar Games' (recent); (c) Series E 2022 press release naming Microsoft, GitHub, Disney as enterprise customers; (d) Sacra and Contrary Research independent corroboration. LIMITATION DOCUMENTED: per the CockroachDB Comcast→Adobe lesson (Atlas memory rule 'live page overrides stale press release', validated 2026-05-09), Pablo browser-side validation of sentry.io/customers/ canonical logo set is REQUIRED before flip to verified — any name in the captured 5 that does NOT appear on the live customer page on data_collection_date must be replaced with a name visible on the live page. The 2024 press release source for Microsoft/Disney/Cloudflare/Reddit IS within the 12-24 month staleness threshold (~1.5 years old), so the staleness risk is lower than the Comcast 3-year-old PRNewswire that failed for CockroachDB; nonetheless, browser validation is the canonical evidence per the live-page-wins rule.

Sentry official 2024 press release on enterprise growth (200% increase, 18K+ paid customers) explicitly names: 'Sentry's offerings support Microsoft, Atlassian, Disney, Nike, Cloudflare, Autodesk, PayPal, Reddit, Peloton and many more of the world's most well-known brands.' Independent corroboration of the 5 captured names (Microsoft, Disney, Cloudflare, Reddit appear in this list; Slack appears in the parallel 2024 blog post). The change is a quota redistribution, not a base-price change — per METHODOLOGY 7.17 cloud_entry_price_usd_month captures the base price floor which has remained stable. metadata.notes: Phase 1-7 complete as of 2026-05-09 (data collection date). Day-2 sanity + flip pending. KEY METHODOLOGY DECISIONS FOR PHASE 1: (1) founded_year=2012 per METHODOLOGY 7.1 strict (year of legal incorporation of the jurisdictional entity that currently operates the company = Functional Software, Inc. dba Sentry). Sentry originated in 2008 as David Cramer's unlicensed Django plugin / open-source project, and the current LICENSE.md (browser-validated by Pablo 2026-05-09) declares 'Copyright 2008-2024 Functional Software, Inc. dba Sentry'. The 2008 start of the copyright claim reflects the original year of the open-source code, assigned to the entity at incorporation; NOT the year the entity itself was incorporated. Atlas uses 2012 because the field tracks the legal entity, not the codebase origin. Triangulated from: (a) cra.mr/sentry-from-the-beginning/ (David Cramer founder narrative — Heroku AddOn launch end-of-2012, 'David founded Sentry in January 2012'); (b) cio.com Jan 2020 piece referencing '8 years since incorporation as of January 2020' (2020 - 8 = 2012). CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM. Exact Delaware Division of Corporations incorporation certificate for Functional Software, Inc. was NOT retrieved during Phase 1 (Atlas Free Preview egress allowlist does not include sec.gov / Delaware corporate registry endpoints; Crunchbase pages also outside cowork-egress). TODO post-Free-Preview: validate founded_year=2012 against the Delaware certificate of incorporation; if exact incorporation date differs (e.g., 2011 if entity was registered before product launch, or different month within 2012), update with HIGH confidence. METHODOLOGY 7.1 NOT amended — this case is canonical 7.1 application, not an edge requiring rule extension. Pablo ruling on 2026-05-09: 2012 over 2008 to maintain Atlas consistency with MongoDB (founded_year=2007 = 10gen Inc. incorporation) and CockroachDB (founded_year=2015 = Cockroach Labs Inc. incorporation), both of which apply 7.1 strictly to legal entities, not to project origins. (2) hq_city='San Francisco' per METHODOLOGY 7.2 canonical short form (no 'San Francisco, CA' redundancy). Cross-confirmed against Crunchbase, Sacra, Tracxn, sentry.io official Series E press release. (3) total_raised_usd=USD 217M derived per METHODOLOGY 7.4 (private-company variant). Manual sum of disclosed rounds: Seed (1.5M, 2015) + A (9M, 2016) + B (16.5M, 2018) + C (40M, 2019) + D (60M, 2021) + E (90M, 2022) = 217M EXACTLY. No implied undisclosed seed/pre-A round (contrast with CockroachDB which had a 6.4M reconciliation gap). Four independent DBs (PitchBook 2026, Sacra, Crunchbase, Tracxn 2026) converge on 217M. CONFIDENCE: HIGH. (4) last_valuation_usd=USD 3B reflects post-money declared in Series E (May 2022). The press release language 'more than $3 billion' is rounded to the nearest billion as the published headline figure; precise post-money was reported as approximately USD 3B by tech press and private-market DBs. CONFIDENCE: HIGH on the headline figure with documented imprecision (the 'more than' qualifier means the exact post-money may have been slightly above 3B, e.g., 3.05B-3.2B; private-company convention captures the rounded headline). Caveat: snapshot is ~4 years old at data_collection_date; SaaS valuation reset 2022-2023 may have impacted real fair value, but no public down-round, secondary tender, or revaluation event has been reported. Interpret as 'most recent disclosed post-money', not 'current fair value' (analog to CockroachDB handling). (5) Source.type='other' used for Crunchbase and Sacra (private-market data providers) consistent with prior Tracxn/Forge Global handling in MongoDB and CockroachDB records. Schema TODO section 8 captures the proposed v1.1 enum addition 'fundraising-database' / 'private-market-data'. (6) FSL-related decisions deferred to Phase 2. Pre-research completed 2026-05-09 with all six A-F decisions validated by Pablo before opening this file (timeline: 2008 unlicensed → 2009 BSD-3-Clause → 2019-11-06 BSL-1.1 → 2023-11-20 FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0). METHODOLOGY 7.18 added as standalone hygiene commit (commit c820708) before Phase 1 began, codifying the time-delayed conversion rule for FSL/BSL families. controlled-vocabularies.yaml updated in the same commit to fix a vocabulary drift (FSL-1.1-Apache → FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0). KEY METHODOLOGY DECISIONS FOR PHASE 2: (7) primary_current='FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0' per METHODOLOGY 7.18, browser-validated by Pablo on 2026-05-09 against the literal LICENSE.md 'Abbreviation:' field at github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/master/LICENSE.md. Each Sentry release independently converts to Apache-2.0 on its second anniversary (per-version conversion, 24-month delay, post-conversion = Apache-2.0). HEAD/latest GA at data_collection_date is FSL — that is the canonical license per 7.18. A user who deliberately checks out a release tag from >24 months ago is using a converted Apache-2.0 version, but per 7.18 that does not redefine primary_current. (8) primary_first='BSD-3-Clause' (SPDX-canonical short form, schema vocabulary at controlled-vocabularies.yaml line 139). The 2008 unlicensed period (~12 months from project origin to 2009 BSD-3-Clause adoption) is documented here in metadata.notes but NOT included as a license_history entry per Pablo ruling 2026-05-09: 'unlicensed' is not in the schema license vocabulary and adds noise without analytical value. license_history therefore captures 2 entries (BSD-3-Clause → BSL-1.1 in 2019-11; BSL-1.1 → FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 in 2023-11), matching the entity-level legal-license history. (9) The 2019-11-06 BSL-1.1 transition source uses commit ca8674c ('meta(LICENSE): Switch to Business Source License 1.1 (#15470)') as the canonical commit per METHODOLOGY 7.8. The official blog announcement (blog.sentry.io/relicensing-sentry/) is referenced for rationale_public. Rationale: same week / same day as the GitHub commit; David Cramer's blog post explicitly cites Cockroach Labs' June 2019 BSL adoption as inspiration. Sentry's chosen BSL configuration was 36-month conversion to Apache-2.0 (BSL allows licensees to set their own change-date). (10) The 2023-11-20 FSL-1.1-Apache-2.0 transition source uses the official Cockroach-Labs-style fallback per METHODOLOGY 7.7 hierarchy item (1): blog.sentry.io/introducing-the-functional-source-license-freedom-without-free-riding/ as the canonical announcement, with TechCrunch / The Register / InfoQ as independent corroboration per 3.2. The exact GitHub commit that physically replaced LICENSE.md was NOT extracted during this data collection: github.com commits-history HTML pages render dynamically and web_fetch returned boilerplate without commit listings; api.github.com is outside the cowork-egress allowlist; `gh` CLI is not installed in the sandbox. Per METHODOLOGY 7.8, commit URL is the 'strongest primary source' but explicitly acknowledged as ideal-not-required when the company blog is canonical (same constraint and resolution as CockroachDB 2019 BSL commit — see CockroachDB metadata.notes item 10). TODO post-Free-Preview: extract 2023 FSL commit hash via local clone of getsentry/sentry (`git log --follow --oneline LICENSE.md | head -50`) and add as additional github-license-file source entry. (11) dual_license=false per METHODOLOGY 7.18 explicit application: time-delayed automatic conversion (FSL per-version 24-month conversion to Apache-2.0) does NOT count as dual licensing because it is NOT user-selectable concurrent. The conversion happens automatically as a function of release age; the user does not choose between FSL and Apache at any given moment. This is the canonical Atlas application of 7.18 to its first qualifying record. (12) cla_required=false with MEDIUM CONFIDENCE based on absence-of-evidence across METHODOLOGY 7.10 hierarchy: (a) out-of-band company legal page — NOT found at sentry.io/legal/ (only DPA); (b) visible CLA bot — NO cla-assistant.io/getsentry/* endpoint surfaced; no CLA Assistant or EasyCLA bot in repo workflows; (c) CONTRIBUTING.md (browser-fetched 2026-05-09 via web_fetch on github.com) — only 11 lines / 8 LOC / 379 bytes, two sections (Feedback / Contributing), NO mention of CLA, DCO, sign-off, or contributor agreement; (d) DCO — NOT mentioned. Conclusion: cla_required=false is the most consistent reading. Methodologically distinct from MongoDB (out-of-band CLA via legal page = HIGH confidence true) and CockroachDB (CLA Assistant bot integration = HIGH confidence true): Sentry takes a third position. This is consistent with Sentry's public anti-CLA posture — the FSL design rationale explicitly cites avoiding the contributor-permanent-assignment problem of traditional CLAs by relying on time-delayed Apache-2.0 conversion to release contributor work. The MEDIUM confidence (vs HIGH) reflects the absence-of-evidence nature of the conclusion: a sample PR review by Pablo on a recent getsentry/sentry pull request would confirm the absence of CLA-bot intervention and upgrade to HIGH. TODO post-Free-Preview: Pablo browser-validate one or two recent PRs in getsentry/sentry to confirm no CLA bot triggers; also check develop.sentry.dev internal docs (linked from CONTRIBUTING.md) for any contributor-agreement language not surfaced in the public CONTRIBUTING.md. (13) CROSS-RECORD OBSERVATION (cla_required pattern emerging across 3 verified records): MongoDB has out-of-band CLA via legal page; CockroachDB has CLA Assistant bot; Sentry has neither — three distinct positions across three records. This is a methodologically rich early signal: cla_required is not converging to a single 'open-core company default' pattern. If subsequent records continue to show heterogeneity, the field will be analytically valuable for Hypothesis 5 (or whichever hypothesis tests contributor-friction structures). If subsequent records cluster into 1-2 dominant patterns and Sentry remains an outlier, it becomes a falsification signal worth examining. KEY METHODOLOGY DECISIONS FOR PHASE 3: (14) commercial_model.type='hybrid' is the consensus fit across two converging arguments: (a) METHODOLOGY 2.1 explicitly lists Sentry as a canonical example of 'hybrid' (the methodology was authored before Sentry's 2023 FSL transition, but the multi-offering structure has not changed); (b) Sacra and Contrary Research both classify Sentry's business as 'hybrid open-source and self-serve SaaS'. EDGE CASE: post-2023-11-20 FSL transition, Sentry has NO OSS layer in the strict OSI sense at HEAD/latest GA release — same structural problem encountered with CockroachDB post-CSL (which forced 'hybrid' as least-bad fit; documented in CockroachDB metadata.notes item 14). The OSS layer DOES emerge per-version after 24 months via FSL's auto-conversion, but per METHODOLOGY 7.18 primary_current measures HEAD which is FSL. Sentry classified 'hybrid' as least-bad fit, reinforcing the schema TODO (METHODOLOGY section 8) for a v1.1 'source-available-tiered' enum value to capture the FSL/CSL/SSPL/Elastic-2.0 pattern. NOTE on multi-repo license heterogeneity: getsentry/sentry main repo is FSL, but Sentry SDKs (sentry-python, sentry-javascript, sentry-java, sentry-cocoa, sentry-ruby, sentry-laravel, sentry-react-native, sentry-dotnet, etc.) are Apache-2.0 or MIT permissive — intentional product strategy because SDKs embed in user code and need permissive licenses for adoption. The Atlas captures the canonical primary_repo (getsentry/sentry, FSL) per precedent (MongoDB main = mongodb/mongo, CockroachDB main = cockroachdb/cockroach), but the SDKs being permissive is a meaningful commercial-strategy fact and is documented at the commercial_model.sources verification_method level rather than as a license_history fact. (15) commercial_model.primary_revenue_stream='cloud' per Sacra's 70% / 30% split (self-serve Cloud SaaS dominant, enterprise + other = 30%). CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM. Single private-market research source (Sacra) for the exact percentage — Contrary Research reports the same 70% but it is likely citing the same upstream data. Sentry has NOT publicly disclosed its revenue mix; the 70%/30% figure is a third-party analyst estimate, not company-disclosed. Sacra additionally cites unverified ARR estimate of USD 128M at end of 2023 with 30% YoY growth as scale context, NOT a high-confidence claim (both Sacra and Contrary mark ARR figures as estimates). (16) CROSS-RECORD OBSERVATION (primary_revenue_stream pattern emerging at N=3): MongoDB='cloud' (Atlas SaaS dominant); CockroachDB='enterprise-license' (self-hosted Enterprise majority); Sentry='cloud' (70% Cloud SaaS). Sentry matches MongoDB pattern, distinct from CockroachDB pattern. The N=3 signal is consistent with a category-level hypothesis: databases (which compete with cloud-provider managed services) skew toward self-hosted Enterprise revenue because cloud-provider competition makes self-hosted licensing the differentiated channel; observability and developer-tools (which compete with API-based SaaS like Datadog/New Relic) skew toward Cloud SaaS revenue because the SaaS form factor IS the product. This early hypothesis is testable as more records are verified — if Grafana (observability) shows cloud-dominant primary_revenue_stream, signal strengthens; if Camunda or BPM tools show self-hosted-dominant, the category hypothesis fragments and individual product-market-fit dynamics dominate. Methodologically meaningful early signal worth tracking. (17) Sentry's revenue model design — 'free for non-Competing-Use' under FSL with NO USD revenue threshold — is materially distinct from CockroachDB's CSL Free (USD 10M revenue cap). This means: a USD 50M-revenue startup running self-hosted Sentry with no commercial competing offering pays $0 to Sentry (compliant with FSL Permitted Purpose), while a USD 50M-revenue company running self-hosted CockroachDB pays the per-CPU CSL Enterprise rate. The functional difference: CockroachDB monetizes on customer scale (revenue threshold); Sentry monetizes on customer use case (Cloud SaaS convenience + enterprise SLAs). This mechanical difference explains why CockroachDB primary_revenue_stream='enterprise-license' (the License Key issuance is the ALL-self-hosted-customer channel post-2024) while Sentry primary_revenue_stream='cloud' (self-hosted is largely free, paid customers self-select into Cloud). KEY METHODOLOGY DECISIONS FOR PHASE 4: (18) cloud_entry_price_usd_month=USD 26/mo per METHODOLOGY 7.17 (lowest fixed-price paid GA Cloud tier). Sentry's three GA paid tiers as of 2026-05-09: Team (USD 26/mo annual base or USD 29/mo monthly base), Business (USD 80/mo annual or USD 89/mo monthly), Enterprise (sales-led custom). Atlas convention: capture the annual-commit headline (USD 26/mo Team) which is what sentry.io presents prominently; the monthly-billing variant (USD 29/mo) is documented here. CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM pending Pablo browser validation of sentry.io/pricing pre-Phase-7 (analog to CockroachDB pricing browser validation 2026-05-05). All eight aggregator sources surveyed (vendr, g2, costbench, sentrypricing.com, last9, bugsly, nurbak, capterra) converge on USD 26/mo Team annual, with sentry.zendesk.com 2025-08-27 plan-change FAQ confirming the USD 26 annual / USD 29 monthly base unchanged across the August 2025 event-volume restructuring. NO non-GA tier flagging (no PREVIEW/BETA on any tier as of data collection). (19) pricing_model='hybrid' per METHODOLOGY 7.11 (multi-dimensional canonical bill structure). Sentry combines: plan tier base (compute/seat/feature dimension), event volume (errors + transactions/spans + replays + attachments, all with included monthly quotas + overage at ~USD 0.00025/error and ~USD 0.000025/transaction), and data retention (default 30 days; longer retention available as add-on/tier upgrade). Three obligatory dimensions → 'hybrid' enum applied. CROSS-RECORD pricing_model OBSERVATION (N=3): MongoDB pricing_model='hybrid' (compute + storage + egress + backup), CockroachDB pricing_model='hybrid' (compute + storage + egress + add-ons), Sentry pricing_model='hybrid' (tier + events + retention). All three records pricing_model='hybrid'; the OSS-with-Cloud-SaaS commercial pattern naturally produces multi-dimensional billing as the SaaS layer scales beyond a single base price. This is a CONVERGING signal (vs the diverging cla_required signal at N=3). Methodologically: 'hybrid' as the dominant pricing_model is consistent with the early Atlas thesis that meaningful open-core companies necessarily evolve multi-dimensional pricing — but if subsequent records (especially services-support or saas-with-oss-marketing types) show non-hybrid pricing_model, the convergence may be artifact of the three early hybrid-type picks (MongoDB / CockroachDB / Sentry are all classified hybrid by 2.1, so their pricing_model convergence is not surprising). (20) Sentry self_hosted_restrictions block documents the structural distinction from CockroachDB CSL: Sentry FSL has NO License Key, NO mandatory Telemetry, NO revenue threshold. This is materially closer to a permissive license in functional permissiveness than to a typical source-available license — distinguished primarily by (a) the explicit Competing Use restriction and (b) the 24-month delay to literal Apache-2.0. Atlas Hypothesis 1 testing (license migration patterns) is enriched by this contrast: CockroachDB and Sentry both pivoted from OSS-permissive → BSL → source-available proprietary, but they configured the resulting source-available license fundamentally differently (CockroachDB tightened gating with License Key + Telemetry + revenue cap; Sentry left it functionally permissive with only the Competing Use carve-out). Same pivot direction, dramatically different end-state product. (21) self_serve_ceiling_usd=null per Atlas convention for event-based pricing: the actual top-of-self-serve depends on event volume consumed (a Business customer with high event volume could pay multiple thousands per month via overage, all self-served). Setting null is more honest than synthesizing a non-existent fixed ceiling. Documented in pricing.sources verification_method. KEY METHODOLOGY DECISIONS FOR PHASE 5: (22) BREAKING THE 'OBSERVABLE 5+2+1' PATTERN. Sentry feature_gating values: sso_saml=commercial, rbac_fine_grained=commercial, audit_logs=commercial, sla_support_tier=commercial, multi_region_ha='oss' (NOT commercial), white_label='na', advanced_analytics='partial', api_rate_limits='na'. Resulting pattern '4 commercial + 2 na + 1 partial + 1 oss' DIFFERS from the MongoDB and CockroachDB convergent pattern '5 commercial + 2 na + 1 partial' in exactly ONE field: multi_region_ha. The deviation is genuine semantic, not artifact: in databases, multi_region_ha measures cluster cross-region survival goals (REGION_FAILURE / ZONE_FAILURE) which are technically gated as Enterprise features at MongoDB Atlas Dedicated and at CockroachDB CSL Enterprise; in observability, multi_region_ha measures data residency / regional ingestion architecture, and Sentry has DEMOCRATIZED data residency choice (US vs EU Frankfurt) across all Cloud tiers including the free Developer plan at no extra cost (per docs.sentry.io/organization/data-storage-location/ and sentry.zendesk.com EU Region FAQ explicit statement). Self-hosted FSL deployments additionally have unrestricted multi-region freedom. Atlas applies the field with category-aware semantics rather than forcing the database interpretation. This is the FIRST emergent example in the dataset of category-specific feature_gating differentiation; if Grafana (also observability) confirms multi_region_ha='oss' or 'partial' in its Phase 5, the database-vs-observability semantic split solidifies as a methodological pattern; if Grafana shows multi_region_ha='commercial' (database-style gating), Sentry's break is observability-product-specific rather than category-wide. Methodologically valuable signal worth tracking. (27) STRUCTURAL INSIGHT (load-bearing for Atlas methodology): Sentry shows 21 releases in L12M but 21,099 commits in L12M — 6.3× fewer releases than CockroachDB (21 vs 133) but 52% MORE commits (21,099 vs 13,843). This proves directly that releases_l12m measures RELEASE STRATEGY, not DEVELOPMENT ACTIVITY. CockroachDB's 7-active-release-branch backport machine inflates releases_l12m via patch backports across parallel maintenance lines; Sentry's calendar-versioned single-trunk produces fewer releases despite higher commit volume. For hypotheses about real development velocity (e.g., Hypothesis 6 development velocity vs commercial outcome correlation), commits_l12m is the honest metric; releases_l12m measures release management strategy and is conflate-vulnerable. CANDIDATE METHODOLOGY 7.19 / 7.20 amendment proposed for post-Free-Preview hygiene commit: 'releases_l12m measures release cadence, not development activity. Use commits_l12m as the canonical development-velocity metric; treat releases_l12m as a release-management-strategy signal that is sensitive to project-level conventions (multi-branch backport vs single-trunk calendar versioning) which can produce 6× variance independent of underlying activity.' This insight only crystallizes at N=3 with a calendar-versioned single-trunk record like Sentry — neither MongoDB nor CockroachDB alone could surface it because both use multi-branch RC patterns. The contrast is the diagnostic. (28) RELEASE CADENCE PATTERNS CROSS-RECORD N=3: MongoDB 97 releases L12M / 1484 lifetime / 6.5% / multi-branch (8.0, 7.0, 6.0+) RC + alpha tags per branch; CockroachDB 133 / 932 / 14.3% / multi-branch (7 active release branches) RCs + alphas + nightly; Sentry 21 / 481 / 4.4% / Calendar YY.MM.patch single-trunk. CockroachDB is the highest L12M-recency outlier (14.3% of all-time tags happened in last year) — methodologically meaningful because the contrast against MongoDB (6.5%) and Sentry (4.4%) frames CockroachDB as the highest-velocity record by that metric, a position not fully explained by underlying activity (CockroachDB's commits_l12m of 13,843 is LOWER than Sentry's 21,099). The asymmetry is structural release-management, not developer effort. Atlas v1 captures both metrics so this conflation can be detected; future v2 could add a derived 'release_cadence_strategy' field (categorical: single-trunk-calver / single-trunk-semver / multi-branch-rc / multi-branch-backport) to make it queryable. (29) PROJECT-AGE TIMESTAMP TRIANGULATION: Phase 1 documented Sentry's three distinct timestamps (2008 code origin, 2012 legal entity incorporation). Phase 6 surfaces a fourth: 2010-08-30 GitHub repo creation (per `gh repo view createdAt`). The repo creation precedes the legal entity by ~2 years and follows the code origin by ~2 years — consistent with a side-project-to-startup arc where the code lived in private/local repos until the project became serious enough for a public GitHub presence (2010), and only became a legal entity (2012) after open-source community traction was established. This 4-timestamp structure (code origin → public repo → legal entity → first VC round 2015) is methodologically distinct from MongoDB (legal entity 2007 = same year as project start) and CockroachDB (legal entity 2015 = same year as project start). Sentry's open-source-first arc is structurally rare in the dataset and may correlate with its FSL adoption rationale (the company explicitly cites avoiding contributor-permanent-assignment, consistent with founders who internalized the open-source community's expectations during the long unfunded period).
